questions.prompt: How does a Docker image differ from a container?

answers.text: Multiple containers can be created from one image.

feedback.legit: Correct. Multiple containers can be created from one image.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Multiple containers can be created from one image.

answers.text: An image is an immutable template used to create containers.

feedback.legit: Correct. An image is an immutable template used to create containers.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. An image is an immutable template used to create containers.

answers.text: A container is a runtime instance created from an image.

feedback.legit: Correct. A container is a runtime instance created from an image.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. A container is a runtime instance created from an image.

answers.text: To persist data outside a container's writable layer.

feedback.legit: Correct. To persist data outside a container's writable layer.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. To persist data outside a container's writable layer.

answers.text: To bake source code permanently into an image.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Image build instructions add files to images; volumes hold runtime data.
feedback.sus: Correct. Image build instructions add files to images; volumes hold runtime data.

answers.text: A volume can survive deletion of the container that used it.

feedback.legit: Correct. A volume can survive deletion of the container that used it.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. A volume can survive deletion of the container that used it.

questions.prompt: Does exposing a port in a Dockerfile publish it to the host?

answers.text: `docker run -p` can map a host port to a container port.

feedback.legit: Correct. `docker run -p` can map a host port to a container port.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. `docker run -p` can map a host port to a container port.

answers.text: A port must appear in EXPOSE before `docker run -p` can publish it.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Runtime port publishing can map a container port even when the Dockerfile does not declare EXPOSE.
feedback.sus: Correct. Runtime port publishing can map a container port even when the Dockerfile does not declare EXPOSE.

answers.text: Yes, EXPOSE automatically creates a host mapping.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Publishing requires a runtime port mapping; EXPOSE is metadata/documentation.
feedback.sus: Correct. Publishing requires a runtime port mapping; EXPOSE is metadata/documentation.

questions.prompt: Why copy dependency manifests before application source in many Dockerfiles?

answers.text: A change to an earlier layer invalidates reuse of later layers.

feedback.legit: Correct. A change to an earlier layer invalidates reuse of later layers.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. A change to an earlier layer invalidates reuse of later layers.

answers.text: The dependency-install layer can remain cached when only source files change.

feedback.legit: Correct. The dependency-install layer can remain cached when only source files change.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. The dependency-install layer can remain cached when only source files change.

answers.text: The ordering is a cache optimization, not a network rule.

feedback.legit: Correct. The ordering is a cache optimization, not a network rule.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. The ordering is a cache optimization, not a network rule.

questions.prompt: How does a bind mount differ from a named volume?

answers.text: A bind mount maps a specific host path into the container.

feedback.legit: Correct. A bind mount maps a specific host path into the container.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. A bind mount maps a specific host path into the container.

answers.text: A bind mount is stored only inside the image.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Bind mounts reference host filesystem paths at runtime.
feedback.sus: Correct. Bind mounts reference host filesystem paths at runtime.

answers.text: Named volumes are managed through Docker's volume storage.

feedback.legit: Correct. Named volumes are managed through Docker's volume storage.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Named volumes are managed through Docker's volume storage.

questions.prompt: Why use a multi-stage Docker build?

answers.text: Every stage runs as a separate production container.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Stages are build phases; only selected outputs need to reach the final image.
feedback.sus: Correct. Stages are build phases; only selected outputs need to reach the final image.

answers.text: A multi-stage build automatically updates every base image to its newest version.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Base-image selection and update policy remain explicit build concerns.
feedback.sus: Correct. Base-image selection and update policy remain explicit build concerns.

answers.text: Build tools can stay in an earlier stage while only runtime artifacts enter the final image.

feedback.legit: Correct. Build tools can stay in an earlier stage while only runtime artifacts enter the final image.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Build tools can stay in an earlier stage while only runtime artifacts enter the final image.

questions.prompt: Why does PID 1 behavior matter inside a container?

answers.text: An init process can help with signal forwarding and zombie reaping.

feedback.legit: Correct. An init process can help with signal forwarding and zombie reaping.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. An init process can help with signal forwarding and zombie reaping.

answers.text: The main process needs correct signal handling and must reap child processes when it creates them.

feedback.legit: Correct. The main process needs correct signal handling and must reap child processes when it creates them.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. The main process needs correct signal handling and must reap child processes when it creates them.

answers.text: PID 1 cannot receive termination signals.

feedback.legit: Not quite. PID 1 can receive signals, but the process and entrypoint must forward or handle them correctly.
feedback.sus: Correct. PID 1 can receive signals, but the process and entrypoint must forward or handle them correctly.

questions.prompt: Is an image environment variable a safe place for a permanent secret?

answers.text: Yes, image configuration is invisible to anyone who pulls it.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Image metadata and layers can expose embedded values.
feedback.sus: Correct. Image metadata and layers can expose embedded values.

answers.text: Secrets should be injected through an appropriate runtime secret mechanism.

feedback.legit: Correct. Secrets should be injected through an appropriate runtime secret mechanism.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Secrets should be injected through an appropriate runtime secret mechanism.

answers.text: Removing a secret in a later layer may not remove it from earlier image history.

feedback.legit: Correct. Removing a secret in a later layer may not remove it from earlier image history.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Removing a secret in a later layer may not remove it from earlier image history.

questions.prompt: What does a Docker health check indicate?

answers.text: That every downstream dependency is permanently available.

feedback.legit: Not quite. A health command only tests what it was designed to observe.
feedback.sus: Correct. A health command only tests what it was designed to observe.

answers.text: A Docker health check by itself always restarts an unhealthy container.

feedback.legit: Not quite. A health check reports status; restart behavior requires an orchestrator or other policy.
feedback.sus: Correct. A health check reports status; restart behavior requires an orchestrator or other policy.

answers.text: Whether a configured command considers the container healthy.

feedback.legit: Correct. Whether a configured command considers the container healthy.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Whether a configured command considers the container healthy.

questions.prompt: Does basic Compose `depends_on` prove a dependency is ready to serve requests?

answers.text: Health-based conditions can improve orchestration when supported and configured.

feedback.legit: Correct. Health-based conditions can improve orchestration when supported and configured.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Health-based conditions can improve orchestration when supported and configured.

answers.text: Yes, process start order guarantees application readiness.

feedback.legit: Not quite. A started container may still be initializing; readiness needs a health condition or retry logic.
feedback.sus: Correct. A started container may still be initializing; readiness needs a health condition or retry logic.

answers.text: Applications should tolerate dependency startup delays.

feedback.legit: Correct. Applications should tolerate dependency startup delays.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Applications should tolerate dependency startup delays.

questions.prompt: How do containers discover each other on a user-defined Docker network?

answers.text: They can use Docker-provided name resolution for container or service names.

feedback.legit: Correct. They can use Docker-provided name resolution for container or service names.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. They can use Docker-provided name resolution for container or service names.

answers.text: They must all publish ports to the public host interface.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Publishing is unnecessary for container-to-container traffic on the shared network.
feedback.sus: Correct. Publishing is unnecessary for container-to-container traffic on the shared network.

answers.text: Network isolation controls which containers can communicate directly.

feedback.legit: Correct. Network isolation controls which containers can communicate directly.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Network isolation controls which containers can communicate directly.

questions.prompt: Why run a container process as a non-root user?

answers.text: It makes host kernel vulnerabilities impossible.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Least privilege reduces impact but does not eliminate container escape risks.
feedback.sus: Correct. Least privilege reduces impact but does not eliminate container escape risks.

answers.text: A non-root process cannot listen on any network port.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Non-root processes can listen on unprivileged ports and may receive narrowly configured capabilities when needed.
feedback.sus: Correct. Non-root processes can listen on unprivileged ports and may receive narrowly configured capabilities when needed.

answers.text: A Dockerfile USER instruction automatically changes ownership of files copied earlier.

feedback.legit: Not quite. USER selects the account for later instructions and runtime; file ownership must be set separately, such as with COPY --chown.
feedback.sus: Correct. USER selects the account for later instructions and runtime; file ownership must be set separately, such as with COPY --chown.
